title: STL Iterators
description: A uniform way to walk through any container's elements: begin(), end(), and why containers without [] still need to be traversable.
objectives: Understand an iterator as a generalized pointer into a container
Use begin() and end() to walk through a container
Understand why iterators matter for containers without []

sections.text: 1. Recap: Indexing Worked Fine for vector

This worked because vector, like an ordinary array from Arrays, stores its elements back to back and supports direct indexing. Not every STL container can do this — a list (a linked list, previewed in STL Containers) has no meaningful way to jump straight to "element 5" without walking through the ones before it.
An iterator is an object that points to a position within a container and can be used to access that element and move to the next one, working consistently across many different container types.

Recall Pointers: an iterator behaves a lot like a pointer — it can be dereferenced with * to get the value it points to, and moved forward with ++. In fact, for something like vector, an iterator often is essentially a pointer under the hood.

end() does not point to the last element — it points to one position past it, a marker meaning "nothing more here." This mirrors the zero-based indexing rule from Arrays, where a loop runs i < size, not i <= size.

sections.code:
cout << *it;  // dereference — get the value the iterator points to (10)
it++;          // move to the next element

sections.title: Comparing this to the index-based loop
points: it starts at begin() — the first element, like i = 0
The loop continues while it != end() — notice != instead of <, since iterators aren't always ordered numbers the way indices are
*it reads the current value, like numbers[i]

sections.text: The exact same it/begin()/end()/*it/it++ pattern works for list, set, map, and nearly every other STL container — even the ones that have no [] operator at all. This is the entire point: one consistent way to traverse any container, regardless of how it actually stores its elements internally.

sections.title: A universal bookmark for any book format
sections.text: Whether a book is a physical paperback, an e-book, or an audiobook, a bookmark still means the same thing: "my current position, and the ability to move to the next part." An iterator is that universal bookmark for any STL container — the container's actual internal shape doesn't matter, the bookmark behaves the same way regardless.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Dereferencing end() — it points past the last element, not to a valid one; *end() is undefined behavior.
Using < to compare iterators in a loop condition instead of != — not every container's iterators support ordering comparisons, but != always works for this pattern.
Assuming [] indexing works on every STL container — it doesn't (list has no []), which is exactly why iterators exist as the universal alternative.
Forgetting to increment the iterator inside a manual loop — leads to an infinite loop, just like forgetting i++ in Loops.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is an iterator?
questions.answer: An object that points to a position within a container, letting you access that element and move to the next one, working consistently across different container types.

questions.question: What do begin() and end() return?
questions.answer: begin() returns an iterator to the first element; end() returns an iterator to one position past the last element, used as a stopping marker rather than a valid element to access.

questions.question: Why is != used instead of < to check an iterator loop's stopping condition?
questions.answer: Because not every container's iterators support ordering comparisons like <, while != reliably works across all of them for this pattern.

questions.question: Why are iterators necessary, given that vector already supports []?
questions.answer: Not every container supports direct indexing — a list has no [] at all — so iterators provide one uniform way to traverse any container, regardless of how it stores its elements internally.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: An iterator is an object that points to a position within a container, dereferenced with * to access the current value and advanced with ++ to move to the next one — behaving much like a pointer. begin() returns an iterator to the first element, and end() returns one pointing past the last element, used as a stopping marker; a typical loop runs while it != end(). This pattern works identically across nearly every STL container, including ones like list that have no [] operator at all, making iterators the universal way to traverse any container regardless of its internal structure.

sections.title: Trick to Remember STL Iterators
sections.text: Iterator = a universal bookmark. begin() = start here. end() = nothing left, stop. *it = read here. it++ = move to the next page.
